title: Repeat Last Ex ({key:@:})
subtitle: {key:@:} replays your last :command. Then {key:@@} repeats again.
summary: @: replays the last Ex command. After running it once, @@ does it again. The same as macro replay — because Ex history is essentially a macro of one command.

blocks.text: Most operators have a repeat key ({key:.}). Ex commands have their own: {key:@}{key::}.

blocks.title: Why It's @ and Not .
blocks.text:
{key:.} replays the last *change* — the last edit that modified the buffer through Normal-mode editing. Ex commands aren't part of that history; they're a separate, parallel stream.

{key:@}{reg} runs the contents of register `reg` as keystrokes. The colon register `:` always holds the last Ex command typed. So {key:@}{key::} just runs that register — the same mechanism as macro playback ({key:@}{key:q}).

After {key:@}{key::}, the macro-replay register becomes `:`, so {key:@}{key:@} (replay last macro) runs the same Ex command again. This is why bulk operations chain naturally: `:s/foo/bar/` then `nnn` (search next) {key:@}{key::} {key:@}{key::} {key:@}{key::}.
